title: Password-Based Authentication
description: Traditional username/password authentication with secure hashing, password policies, and reset flows; suitable for web applications with human users.

cost.costNotes: Password authentication has low infrastructure costs but requires email service for password resets.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Password authentication infrastructure costs are minimal. Min=$0 for self-managed implementation using application database for user credentials with bcrypt/Argon2 hashing, no additional services required for basic implementation. Max=$300 represents production scenarios with supporting services: (1) Email service for password reset flows (SendGrid: 100K emails free, then $15-20/mo for 50K emails; AWS SES: $0.10/1000 emails = $10-50/mo), (2) Rate limiting/anti-fraud (Redis: $30-50/mo, or application-level), (3) Session storage if not using JWT (Redis ElastiCache: $15-30/mo for cache.t3.micro), (4) Optional breach monitoring (Have I Been Pwned API: free for <10 req/sec). Enterprise features like adaptive authentication, risk scoring, or bot detection (reCAPTCHA Enterprise, DataDome) can add $100-500+/mo but are optional enhancements.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium complexity implementation. Password authentication requires: (1) User registration flow with email verification - 12-16 hours, (2) Secure password hashing implementation (Argon2 recommended, bcrypt acceptable) with proper salt generation - 8-12 hours, (3) Login flow with rate limiting and account lockout protection - 12-16 hours, (4) Password reset flow with secure token generation and email delivery - 16-24 hours, (5) Password policy enforcement (length, complexity, common password checks) - 8-12 hours, (6) Session management (secure cookies, CSRF protection) - 12-16 hours, (7) Security features (account lockout, suspicious activity detection) - 12-20 hours, (8) UI/UX for login, registration, reset flows - 16-24 hours. Total: 96-140 developer hours at $150/hour = $14,400-21,000. However, with modern frameworks (Django auth, Passport.js, Spring Security), pre-built UI components, breach detection APIs, and AI coding assistance reducing boilerplate by 60-70%, realistic adoption cost is $2500-4000 for teams with web development experience, representing 15-25 focused development hours.
provenance.licenseCost: Password authentication is a fundamental pattern with no licensing requirements. All major web frameworks include built-in password authentication modules (Django auth system, Rails devise, Spring Security, Laravel auth, ASP.NET Identity) - all free and open-source. Password hashing libraries (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt) are open-source. Email delivery can use free tiers (SendGrid 100/day free, AWS SES $0 for 62K emails if sent from EC2). No commercial licenses or proprietary software required for implementation.

provides.reasoning: This pattern directly implements user authentication through username/password credentials, providing a complete authentication mechanism that can be substituted with other authentication approaches like OAuth or SAML.
The pattern inherently provides credential management capabilities through secure password hashing, storage, validation, policy enforcement, and reset flows.
Password authentication requires maintaining authenticated state after successful login, typically through session tokens or cookies to keep users authenticated across requests.

requires.reasoning: Password authentication requires persistent storage for user credentials, hashed passwords, reset tokens, and authentication metadata across sessions and application restarts.
Secure password hashing algorithms (bcrypt, argon2, PBKDF2) are essential for storing passwords safely and are a fundamental requirement for any secure password-based authentication system.

HTTPS/TLS is required to protect passwords during transmission from client to server, as transmitting credentials over insecure channels completely undermines the security model of password authentication.

supports_nfr.reason: This pattern implements password-based authentication using username/password credentials.
Pattern supports optional audit logging of login attempts, password changes, and authentication events.
Password authentication is compatible with GDPR requirements when combined with proper data handling.
Pattern supports HIPAA compliance when configured with appropriate encryption and access controls.
Password authentication is compatible with CCPA requirements for user data and access management.
Pattern supports SOX compliance when combined with audit logging and access control measures.

supports_constraints.reason: Password authentication is a framework-level pattern that works across all cloud providers and on-premises.
Password authentication is widely supported in web frameworks across all major languages.
Password authentication is designed for applications with human users across web, mobile, and desktop platforms.
Pattern works with or without caching layers for session or credential data.
Password authentication is compatible with applications that use or do not use async messaging.
Pattern is neutral to AI inference capabilities in the application.
Password authentication supports both single-tenant and multi-tenant application architectures.
Pattern is compatible with applications that include or exclude batch processing features.
Password authentication works with or without distributed transaction coordination.
Pattern is neutral to real-time streaming capabilities in the application.
Password authentication is independent of vector search or embedding capabilities.
Pattern works with document-oriented databases or relational databases for credential storage.
Password authentication can use key-value stores for session management but does not require them.
Pattern is independent of graph database usage in the application.
Password authentication is neutral to time-series database requirements.
Pattern supports both OLTP and non-OLTP workload types for credential management.
Password authentication is compatible with applications that include or exclude OLAP workloads.

hash_algorithm.description: Password hashing algorithm. argon2 is recommended (winner of Password Hashing Competition, memory-hard, GPU-resistant), bcrypt is widely supported and battle-tested, scrypt is alternative memory-hard option.

min_password_length.description: Minimum password length in characters. NIST recommends at least 8, but 12+ is better for resistance against brute force. Longer is generally better than complex.

require_complexity.description: Require password complexity (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols). NIST now recommends against this in favor of length and breach checking, but many compliance regimes still require it.

check_breached_passwords.description: Check passwords against Have I Been Pwned database to reject previously breached passwords. Highly recommended security enhancement with minimal UX impact.

account_lockout_threshold.description: Number of failed login attempts before account lockout. Lower values (3-5) improve security but may frustrate legitimate users. Balance security vs usability.

lockout_duration_minutes.description: Account lockout duration after exceeding failed login threshold. Shorter durations (5-15 min) reduce user frustration while still mitigating brute force attacks.

password_reset_token_expiry_hours.description: Password reset token expiration time. Shorter times (1-4 hours) improve security by reducing attack window, but may frustrate users who don't check email immediately.

require_email_verification.description: Require email verification during account registration. Recommended to prevent fake accounts and ensure password reset capability, but adds friction to signup flow.

requires_nfr.reason: Pattern supports high availability up to 99.9% with database replication and session management.
Password hashing (Argon2/bcrypt) intentionally adds computational cost of 100-200ms for security.
P99 latency includes password verification, database lookups, and session creation overhead.
Pattern supports up to 1M logins/hour, limited by intentional CPU-intensive hashing operations.
Password auth enables fast recovery (1-5 minutes) as credentials are stored in database with replication.
With synchronous database writes and replication, RPO can be 0-5 minutes for credential changes.
